summary:
• Community Development Milestone: The East Lake Community Resource Hub is opening, marking Chattanooga's first such facility, aimed at enhancing local support services.
  • Education Initiative: UTC introduces a new program this fall to simplify textbook purchases and reduce costs for students, enhancing educational accessibility.
  • Historic Recognition: St. Marks United Methodist Church has been listed in the National Register of Historic Places, underscoring its cultural significance in North Chattanooga.

items.content_text:
❤️
Locations + more info in the caption ⬇️💦
 With the humid temps this summer (🥵), I thought it’s a good time to re-post this reel with free places to cool off when you’re visiting downtown Chattanooga, TN! 
 These are all great for local families with kids or those sightseeing/visiting while walking around Chattanooga! *Note: pets aren’t allowed in these public water areas
 📍 The Passage (aka the water steps) is an interactive art installation that’s a stairway waterfall cascading into a very shallow wading area next to the Tennessee Aquarium at Ross’s Landing.
 - FREE // pay to park
 - Beautiful riverfront views
 - Unique art honoring Native Americans of the Trail of Tears
 📍Coolidge Park (across the river) has a large fountain-style splash pad with animals that kids love.
 - FREE // pay to park
 - It’s located right next to the vintage carousel (free to ride!) and is a great walking/picnic area.
 - Lots of green space & great views of the river
 📍The Tennessee Aquarium Plaza has a wading stream and splash area with small rocks to climb around on.
 - FREE // pay to park
 - Located right outside of the aquarium (short walk from The Passage)
 - There are also walkways & bridges nearby
 #chattanooga #chattanoogatn #downtownchattanooga #chattanoogatennessee #tennessee

items.title: ⛑️ I may have missed media day… but I wasn’t going to miss the opportunity to see this incredible project up close! 😄 ✅...
items.content_text:
⛑️ I may have missed media day… but I wasn’t going to miss the opportunity to see this incredible project up close! 😄
 ✅ Finally got my hard hat!
 ✅ Took an up-close tour of Chattanooga’s e2i2 Major Wastewater Infrastructure Investment 💧🏗️
 ✅ Got a behind-the-scenes look at one of the largest infrastructure investments in our city’s history.
 ✅ Learned how these improvements will modernize our wastewater system, protect our waterways, support future growth, and improve resiliency for generations to come.
 ✅ Walked away with an even greater appreciation for the talented professionals who keep our city running every single day.
 📸 Credit: Ayanna Bronner
 A huge THANK YOU to Chattanooga’s incredible Wastewater Team for taking the time to show me around and explain the work happening beneath our feet. Your expertise, passion, and commitment to serving Chattanooga do not go unnoticed!
 The best projects aren’t always the most visible—but they’re often the most important. Investing in infrastructure is investing in Chattanooga’s future, and I’m excited to see this transformational work continue.
 #HardHatTour ⛑️ #e2i2 #InfrastructureMatters #Wastewater #Chattanooga BuildingStrongerCommunities

items.title: Every semester, students at UTC purchase textbooks and course materials for their classes. Beginning this fall, they wil...
items.content_text:
Every semester, students at UTC purchase textbooks and course materials for their classes. Beginning this fall, they will have a new option designed to simplify the process and help reduce costs.
 Starting Monday, July 27, the MocsBooks Total Access Program will offer UTC undergraduate students a simpler, potentially more budget-friendly option for buying their textbooks and materials.
 Students will be automatically enrolled in the program and will receive an email 30 days before the start of the semester. The email will include the courses they are enrolled in, what the program covers and an estimate of how much they could save each semester.
 🔗  Learn more here: https://blog.utc.edu/…/introducing-mocsbooks-the-new-way-t…/

Chattanooga’s First Community Resource Hub Is Opening!
 The City of Chattanooga Department of Community Development invites you to celebrate the grand opening of the East Lake Community Resource Hub, the first of six neighborhood-based hubs planned across Chattanooga!
 📅 Monday, July 27, 2026
 🎀 Ribbon Cutting: 2:30 p.m.
 🎉 Community Celebration: 3:00–6:00 p.m.

 🎟️Free and open to the public
 The East Lake Community Resource Hub will serve as a welcoming, one-stop destination where residents and families can connect with:
 ✅ Health and wellness services
 ✅ Educational resources
 ✅ Workforce development opportunities
 ✅ Family support programs
 ✅ Financial and community assistance
 ✅ City services and other valuable resources
 📧 CRH@chattanooga.gov
 📱 Text “Resource” to 423-643-7300
 🌐 https://cha.city/resource

items.title: Congratulations to St. Marks United Methodist Church on its listing in the National Register of Historic Places! 🎉 For...
items.content_text:
Congratulations to St. Marks United Methodist Church on its listing in the National Register of Historic Places! 🎉
 For more than 130 years, St. Marks has been a cornerstone of the North Chattanooga community—a place of worship, fellowship, and service. Founded in 1887, the congregation moved into its current sanctuary in 1913, famously marching through a snowstorm while singing "Onward Christian Soldiers" as they entered their new church home.
 Designed by architect W. H. Sears in the distinctive Spanish Mission style, the church remains one of Chattanooga's most architecturally significant religious landmarks. Its beautiful stucco exterior, prominent tower, and warm Mission-style interior reflect both exceptional craftsmanship and the vision of a community that came together to build a true "Neighborhood Church."
 Listing on the National Register of Historic Places recognizes not only the building's architectural significance but also the generations of people whose faith, generosity, and perseverance have shaped this remarkable place.
 Congratulations to the congregation of St. Marks United Methodist Church and to everyone who helped achieve this well-deserved recognition. Here's to preserving this important piece of Chattanooga's history for generations to come!
 #PreserveChattanooga #NationalRegister #HistoricPreservation #NorthChattanooga #ThisPlaceMatters #ChattanoogaHistory

items.title: Jon Holmes Joins Elite Group of ICMA-Credentialed City Managers in Tennessee WASHINGTON D.C. (July 16, 2026)
items.content_text:
Jon Holmes Joins Elite Group of ICMA-Credentialed City Managers in Tennessee
 WASHINGTON D.C. (July 16, 2026) – Jon Holmes, City Manager of the City of Collegedale, Tennessee, recently received the prestigious Credentialed Manager designation from ICMA, the International City/County Management Association. Mr. Holmes is one of over 1,700 local government management professionals currently credentialed through the ICMA Voluntary Credentialing Program.
 ICMA’s mission is to advance professional local government through leadership, management, innovation, and ethics and by increasing the proficiency of appointed chief administrative officers, assistant administrators, and other employees who serve local governments and regional entities around the world. The organization’s 13,000 members in 27 countries also include educators, students, and other local government employees.
 To receive the prestigious ICMA credential, a member must have significant experience as a senior management executive in local government; have earned a degree, preferably in public administration or a related field; and demonstrated a commitment to high standards of integrity and to lifelong learning and professional development.
 “On behalf of the City Board of Commissioners, we are proud to recognize Jon for earning the ICMA Credentialed Manager designation,” said Commissioner Debbie Baker. “This achievement reflects not only his efforts to work toward professional excellence, but also his deep commitment to ethical leadership, innovation, and continuous improvement in public service. Under Jon’s leadership, we are working to develop and address community priorities, strengthen our entire organization, and better serve all of our residents. This recognition demonstrates his ability to meet the high standard of governance and administration that our community expects and deserves”. Commissioner Laura Howse added “Understanding that only 24 city managers in Tennessee have earned this certification, makes me especially proud, good job Jon.”
 Mr. Holmes is qualified by 18+ years of professional local government executive experience, educational attainment of a Bachelor of Arts Degree from Harding University in History and Political Science and a Master’s in Public Administration from the University of Nebraska. Prior to his appointment in January 2026 as City Manager of Collegedale, he served as City Manager for the City of Aurora, Missouri for over 7 years, as well as serving as a County Administrator in Ottawa, Kansas, a City Administrator in Odessa, Missouri, and a Budget and Management Analyst for the City of Laramie Wyoming. Prior to that he worked as an Assistant Emergency Response and Management Coordinator for the Lincoln – Lancaster County Health Department in Lincoln, Nebraska. He has earned the Certified Municipal Official Certification from the Missouri Municipal League, has served as the Vice-Chair for the Transportation Advisory Committee with the Southwest Missouri Regional Planning Commission, served on the Judicial Nominating Commission for the Kansas 4th Judicial District, and has served on a number of regional planning commissions and other boards and commissions that work for the betterment of municipal governments and the citizens they serve.
 Mr. Holmes has also remained actively engaged in the communities he has served, contributing to organizations such as Rotary International, local chambers of commerce, and numerous other civic groups.
 The Credentialled City Manager designation from ICMA speaks to the high level of professionalism that Mr. Holmes brings to the City Manager position and also underscores the City of Collegedale’s commitment to recruiting and retaining highly qualified and experienced individuals to serve the Citizens of Collegedale.

items.title: This morning, Chief Deputy Spencer Daniels and HCSO personnel joined local first responders, military personnel, elected...
items.content_text:
This morning, Chief Deputy Spencer Daniels and HCSO personnel joined local first responders, military personnel, elected officials, and Hamilton County residents at the Fallen Five Memorial to remember the 11th anniversary of the July 16th, 2015, terrorist attack in Chattanooga. 
 The solemn ceremony served as a time of remembrance and reflection, honoring the lives of Marine Sergeant Carson Holmquist, Navy Logistics Specialist 2nd Class Randall Smith, Marine Gunnery Sergeant Thomas Sullivan, Marine Lance Corporal Squire “Skip” Wells, and Marine Staff Sergeant David Wyatt, who were killed during the terrorist attack at the Naval Reserve Center.
 Their legacy of service and sacrifice will forever be remembered.

items.title: Mayor Weston Wamp joined community leaders, first responders, Gold Star families, and residents this morning to honor th...
items.content_text:
Mayor Weston Wamp joined community leaders, first responders, Gold Star families, and residents this morning to honor the five service members who lost their lives in the July 16, 2015 terrorist attack.

items.title: Co-ordinated governance across water, energy, industry and urban development is essential to accelerate sustainable deve...
items.content_text:
Co-ordinated governance across water, energy, industry and urban development is essential to accelerate sustainable development.
 The new OECD report, Bridging the Gaps for Sustainable Development: Coherent Policies for Water, Energy, Industry and Cities, shows that these sectors are closely linked in practice, but are often planned and managed separately.
 Drawing on country experiences, the report highlights practical ways to strengthen policy coherence and co-ordination across levels of government, including:
 ➡️ better alignment between planning, budgeting and delivery
 ➡️ clearer institutional roles and responsibilities
 ➡️ more systematic use of data
 ➡️ greater capacity to manage trade-offs and spillovers
 The report also shows that integrated approaches can help governments reduce costs, strengthen resilience, and improve the effectiveness of policies across these areas.
 Download the report 🔗 https://brnw.ch/21x4bRv

items.content_text:
Traffic Advisory for Ringgold Road in #EastRidgeTN! 🚧
 Motorists traveling along Ringgold Road should expect traffic delays and changing traffic patterns as Tennessee American Water works on a new water main installation at the intersection of Adair Avenue and Ringgold Road.
 🚦 What to Expect:
 • Eastbound traffic begins merging into a single lane near Keeble Street.  An additional traffic shift occurs at Prater Road.  Traffic returns to normal at S. Smith Street.
 • Westbound traffic will not have access to the center turn lane beginning at Adair Avenue.  Traffic returns to normal at Prater Road.
 🚧 Additional Impacts:
 Temporary road closures and detours may occur on nearby residential streets, including Adair Avenue, Marietta Street, and S. Smith Street. 
 Please use caution when driving in the area.  The project is scheduled to be completed by the end of the business day on Friday, July 17, 2026.
 Thanks for your patience as these infrastructure improvements are completed and for your help in keeping everyone safe on the Ridge.

 Children entering preschool, pre-K, kindergarten, 7th grade, or those enrolling in a Tennessee school for the first time may need to obtain an updated Tennessee Certificate of Immunization.
 The Hamilton County Health Department offers childhood and school immunizations without an appointment. 
 School Shot Records can be requested via https://health.hamiltontn.org/data,…/requestshotrecords.aspx.
 For more information, please visit http://health.hamiltontn.org/…/childschoolimmunizations.aspx) or call (423) 209-8350 and press #2.

items.content_text:
Over the next few weeks, we'd like to introduce you to the people behind the Southeast Tennessee Development District.

The work happening across our region is made possible by a dedicated team committed to serving our communities, building partnerships, and helping move Southeast Tennessee forward. We thought we'd start with the person you'll see (and hear from) the most on our communication channels. 

👋 Hi! I'm Chaz Johnston , Director of Communications & Community Relations at the Southeast Tennessee Development District (SETD).

Since joining the organization, I've had the opportunity to meet community leaders, partners, and organizations from across our 10-county region. And one thing has become abundantly clear to me: Southeast Tennessee is filled with passionate people who are investing their time, talent, and energy into making our communities stronger.

My role is to help tell those stories by showcasing the partnerships, projects, and people whose work often happens behind the scenes but creates a lasting impact throughout our region.

For me, communication is about more than sharing updates. It's about building trust, strengthening and cultivating relationships, and helping people see the value of working together toward a common goal.

Thank you for following along. Over the coming weeks, we'll be introducing you to the incredible people behind SETD and the work they're doing to help shape the future of Southeast Tennessee. Stay tuned. I can't wait for you to meet them!

items.title: The City of Collegedale is pleased to announce that Garrett Haynes has been selected as the new Planning & Community Dev...
items.content_text:
The City of Collegedale is pleased to announce that Garrett Haynes has been selected as the new Planning & Community Development Director. He will begin his role at the end of this month.
 Garrett is a 2015 graduate of the University of Tennessee at Chattanooga (UTC) and brings a diverse background in community planning, regional development, research, and geographic information systems. He joins us from the Southeast Tennessee Development District, where he has served for almost eight years, most recently as a Senior Planner. Prior to that, Garrett gained experience working with local and regional planning organizations in roles focused on GIS, research, and planning support, giving him valuable insight into the planning process and the opportunities and challenges communities face as they grow and develop.
 “We are excited to add Garrett to our Management Team," said City Manager Jon Holmes. "His experience will be a great asset, and the fact that he has worked in the Hamilton County area will help him get up to speed quickly with the great things that are happening in Collegedale."
 Outside of work, Garrett enjoys spending time on the golf course and fishing.
 We are excited to welcome Garrett and look forward to the experience and perspective he will bring to our leadership team!

items.title: From Metro Stations to Urban Places in Riyadh Can a metro station, on its own, create a vibrant, walkable neighbourhood? Twice a w...
items.content_text:
From Metro Stations to Urban Places in Riyadh

Can a metro station, on its own, create a vibrant, walkable neighbourhood?

Twice a week in the lead up to Graduation we’re showcasing the consultancy projects undertaken by participants on LSE Cities' Executive MSc in Cities that formed part of their project work for the Urban Consultancy Project, the final output of the programme. 

Sultan AlKhuraissi, Class of 2024-26, explores how Riyadh Metro can go beyond mobility to shape active, climate-responsive urban districts in a city historically shaped by automobile-oriented growth.

Focusing on three distinct station contexts—KAFD as a central business district, STC/Olaya as a mixed-use corridor, and Qasr AlHokm as a heritage district—the project combines spatial analysis, policy review, expert interviews, and practitioner engagement to move from theory to delivery.

The project reflects a core strength of the Executive MSc in Cities programme: connecting urban theory with practical delivery. Understanding the city as a system means not only diagnosing challenges, but asking what can realistically be delivered, by whom, and with what impact.

Read more here: https://lnkd.in/efchxbHK 

#LSECities #ExecutiveMScInCities

items.title: Shooting – Hawkins Oak Lane The Chattanooga Police Department (CPD) is investigating a domestic-related shooting that oc...
items.content_text:
Shooting – Hawkins Oak Lane
 The Chattanooga Police Department (CPD) is investigating a domestic-related shooting that occurred on July 16, 2026 just prior to 2:00 a.m. at a residence on Hawkins Oak Lane.
 Upon arrival, officers located an adult female with a gunshot wound. She was taken to a local hospital by Hamilton County EMS for treatment of a non-life-threatening injury. 
 The preliminary investigation determined the incident stemmed from a domestic dispute between the victim and suspect. During the encounter, the suspect discharged a firearm, striking the victim. The suspect fled the scene before officers arrived. 
 CPD Special Victims Unit and Crime Scene Units responded to investigate and process the scene. 
 This information is preliminary and subject to change as the investigation continues. Chattanooga Police ask anyone with information regarding this incident to call 423-698-2525.
 #CPDnews

items.title: This summer, our hearts are overflowing with gratitude for the incredible volunteers who joined forces with the N.I.C.E....
items.content_text:
This summer, our hearts are overflowing with gratitude for the incredible volunteers who joined forces with the N.I.C.E. team to love on our community! 💪🏼❤
 We were so inspired by the 100 amazing folks who rolled up their sleeves to completely transform five CNE rental properties with fresh landscaping and paint. Right alongside them, 80 dedicated volunteers from YM360 and Trinity Baptist Church all the way from Philadelphia, Mississippi, hit the pavement to complete BUKI scoring assessments across the entirety of the Oak Grove and Ridgedale neighborhoods.
 But this experience was about so much more than data and assessments. These volunteers trained in the Buki methodology and walked away with hands-on community-building experience to carry back home. What they learned here—that showing up for your neighbors and investing in your community truly changes everything—is a spark we know will ignite this same beautiful mission wherever they go next.
 Thank you all for bringing your energy, your hard work, and your hearts to our neighborhoods this summer! ☀

items.title: HCSO Investigating Juvenile Pedestrian Hit by Vehicle Hamilton County, TN – On the evening of July 15, 2026, HCSO deputi...
items.content_text:
HCSO Investigating Juvenile Pedestrian Hit by Vehicle
 Hamilton County, TN – On the evening of July 15, 2026, HCSO deputies responded to a traffic incident at the 7200 block of Hixson Pike involving a 13-year-old struck by a vehicle.
 Deputies determined a truck pulling a trailer loaded with lawn equipment stopped in a travel lane due to a weed eater becoming loose. The driver advised the juvenile passenger to exit the vehicle and secure the equipment.
 A second vehicle collided with the stopped vehicle while the juvenile was attempting to secure the trailer, and the juvenile was transported to the hospital by ambulance with non-life threatening injuries.
 The HCSO Traffic Unit is investigating, and charges may follow pending the outcome.
 This information is preliminary and subject to change. No further details are available.

items.title: The Tennessee Department of Health recently confirmed the state’s first two measles cases in 20 years. While these cases...
items.content_text:
The Tennessee Department of Health recently confirmed the state’s first two measles cases in 20 years. While these cases are located more than two hours away from Chattanooga, this milestone serves as a helpful reminder of how easily the virus can spread.
 Because measles is highly contagious, it can actually spread before someone even realizes they are sick.
 Key facts to keep in mind:
 Delayed Symptoms: Signs of measles can take days to appear after exposure, but the virus spreads very rapidly in under-vaccinated communities.
 What to Do: If you or a family member ever suspects exposure, contact your healthcare provider right away.
 Stay informed and protected:
 • Know the signs: View the CDC's quick-reference infographic: https://www.cdc.gov/…/measles-isnt-just-a-little-rash-infog…
 • Check your records: If you or your children need to catch up on the MMR vaccine, you can schedule an appointment locally with the Hamilton County Health Department at (423) 209-8340.

items.title: Persistent higher energy prices this year could cut grain output by 26 million metric tonnes in 2027, putting food secur...
items.content_text:
Persistent higher energy prices this year could cut grain output by 26 million metric tonnes in 2027, putting food security at risk for the most vulnerable.
 If conditions stabilise, the report projects a 13% increase in global agricultural and fish production by 2035 - but this growth needs to come with better food system resilience.
 Key priorities: better support for farmers, stronger productivity growth and open, well-functioning global markets.
 Learn more: https://brnw.ch/21x4aRT
 #AgOutlook

items.title: Today, Sheriff Austin Garrett and the men and women of the Hamilton County Sheriff’s Office remember the sacrifice of Ma...
items.content_text:
Today, Sheriff Austin Garrett and the men and women of the Hamilton County Sheriff’s Office remember the sacrifice of Marine Sergeant Carson Holmquist, Navy Logistics Specialist 2nd Class Randall Smith, Marine Gunnery Sergeant Thomas Sullivan, Marine Lance Corporal Squire “Skip” Wells, and Marine Staff Sergeant David Wyatt, who were killed in the line of duty July 16, 2015 during a terrorist attack in Chattanooga.
 We also remember the service and sacrifice of Marine Sergeant DeMonte Cheeley and Chattanooga Police Department Sergeant Dennis Pedigo, Jr., who were also shot and injured in the line of duty during the event.
 Lastly, let us remember the many courageous law enforcement officers and first responders who answered the call without hesitation or fear.
 May we never forget.

items.title: The City of Chattanooga Office of Arts, Culture and Creative Economy is seeking a local vocal ensemble, preferably a cho...
items.content_text:
The City of Chattanooga Office of Arts, Culture and Creative Economy is seeking a local vocal ensemble, preferably a choir, to perform during the opening celebration for our newest public art mural, Summer Serenade, on Friday, July 31, 2026.
 The celebration will take place at City Hall, with the performance beginning at 6:00 PM. We are looking for an unplugged vocal performance lasting approximately 30-45 minutes to help create a memorable and welcoming atmosphere as we celebrate this exciting addition to Chattanooga's public art collection.
 Please note:
 The performance will take place outdoors at City Hall (on the steps).
 No sound equipment or electricity will be available.
 Performances must be fully acoustic (unplugged).
 We are especially interested in choirs and other vocal ensembles, though all vocal groups are welcome to express interest.
 This is a paid opportunity
 If you are interested in participating, please reply to this email no later than 4:30 PM on Monday, July 20, 2026. Please include a brief description of your ensemble, any relevant performance experience, and links to audio or video samples, if available.
 Space is limited, and participation will be based on availability and the needs of the event.
 We hope you'll join us as we celebrate the unveiling of Summer Serenade and an evening of public art, music, and community in the heart of downtown Chattanooga. We look forward to hearing from you! Email Carmen J. Elliott (cdavis@chattanooga.gov) for information.

items.title: The applications of research at UTC range from quantum and the automotive industry to Tennessee’s rivers and streams, an...
items.content_text:
The applications of research at UTC range from quantum and the automotive industry to Tennessee’s rivers and streams, and even to track, field and hardwood.
 Dr. Jenny Hogg, an athletic training professor in the Department of Health and Human Performance, aims to help athletes perform at their best. Using augmented reality and machine learning, Hogg’s research examines athletes’ biomechanical performance and methods to improve it.
 “My research marries hip, knee and ankle biomechanics,” Hogg said. “Examining joints and angles where you’re moving your hip in a way that’s good for your knee and vice versa.”
 🔗  Read more here: https://blog.utc.edu/…/behind-the-research-at-utc-dr-jenny…/

items.title: Mayor Weston Wamp and Commissioner Lee Helton announced a proposal to purchase approximately 8.5 acres in Apison to pres...
items.content_text:
Mayor Weston Wamp and Commissioner Lee Helton announced a proposal to purchase approximately 8.5 acres in Apison to preserve green space and expand opportunities for parks, recreation, and youth sports.
 As one of Hamilton County’s fastest-growing communities, investing in parks today helps ensure families have places to gather, play, and enjoy the outdoors for generations to come.
 The proposal will be considered by the Hamilton County Commission, with a vote anticipated on August 5.

items.title: We have a sneak peek behind the $75 million restoration and expansion of the @tivolichatt — bringing it back to its 1920...
items.content_text:
We have a sneak peek behind the $75 million restoration and expansion of the @tivolichatt — bringing it back to its 1920s glory (with modern upgrades)!
 The entrance and lobby will feature a new box office and guest services area, a bar, new restrooms, an elevator, and a connection point to the new Tivoli Performing Arts Center being built next door. Craftsmen are currently finishing refurbishing plasterwork and leaf detailing before repainting, and all new carpet will be put in that matches the original as well. The stage area will be restored and feature all new seats.
 Next door, the center will have a Patrons Lounge, a year-round restaurant, and the Bobby Stone Theatre showing independent films and acting as a flex event space. The fully accessible space will be connected to the Tivoli at every floor, leading to the balcony area — accompanied by more guest amenities. This building will also house a rooftop bar. 
 The Tivoli is on track to reopen in October with its first show already on the books. The performing arts center is projected to be completed by the end of the year with programming slated for early 2027. Head to @tivolichatt’s website to see more about this restoration project that’s been years in the making!

items.title: Non-compete clauses are widespread across OECD countries and have a negative impact on the growth of workers’ wages. Acr...
items.content_text:
Non-compete clauses are widespread across OECD countries and have a negative impact on the growth of workers’ wages.
 Across 15 OECD countries, 30% of employees are bound by non-compete clauses, contractual terms that prevent workers from joining or starting competing firms. Such clauses reduce both wage and productivity growth.
 Stronger rules alone may not be enough to prevent the misuse of such clauses, as unclear or overly broad terms are still common.
 Governments can improve transparency, simplify regulations and step up enforcement by public authorities, including sanctions for clauses that are too broad. Social partners can also help raise awareness and ensure fair, appropriate use of these clauses. 
 Learn more how non-compete clauses impact on wages and productivity via the link in bio.

Now Accepting Bids in #EastRidgeTN! 
 Springvale Park Redevelopment Project 🐾
 The City of East Ridge is now accepting sealed bids for the Springvale Park Redevelopment Project. This project will transform Springvale Park with new amenities and infrastructure designed to better serve residents and their four-legged companions.
 Project Scope Includes:
 ✔️Site grading and drainage improvements
 ✔️Paving, sidewalks, and amenity pads
 ✔️ Fencing
 🚰 Utility improvements
 🚻 Restroom facilities
 🌳 Landscaping
 The project also includes three (3) add alternate bid items as identified in the Bid Documents.
 🗓️ Pre-Bid Conference
 Monday, July 20, 2026 | 10:00 AM (ET)
 East Ridge City Hall
 1517 Tombras Avenue
 A site visit will immediately follow. Attendance is encouraged.
 🗓️ Bid Opening
 Wednesday, August 5, 2026 | 2:30 PM (ET)
 East Ridge City Hall
 1517 Tombras Avenue
 📌 Bid Submission
 Sealed bids must be delivered to:
 City of East Ridge
 ATTN: City Clerk's Office
 1517 Tombras Avenue
 East Ridge, TN 37412
 Clearly label the envelope:
 "Springvale Redevelopment Project – East Ridge"
 Bids must be received no later than 2:30 PM (ET) on Wednesday, August 5, 2026.
 📄 Project Plans & Bid Documents

items.title: Tennessee's New Drink Spiking Law is Now in Effect As of July 1, 2026, intentionally spiking someone’s drink, food, or m...
items.content_text:
Tennessee's New Drink Spiking Law is Now in Effect
 As of July 1, 2026, intentionally spiking someone’s drink, food, or medication to cause involuntary intoxication is a felony in Tennessee.
 We’ve received questions from bars, restaurants, and community partners about what this new law means. One of the biggest misconceptions? The law does not require alcohol-serving establishments to purchase or provide drink testing devices. That requirement was removed before the bill became law.
 Preventing drink spiking requires a comprehensive approach—including staff training, active bystander intervention, monitoring unattended drinks, and creating a culture of safety.
 If you have questions about the law, drink testing devices, or prevention strategies, we’re here to help.
 📍 Sexual Assault Center
 Atarah A.M. Smith, MS
 Community Relations Manager
 📧 ataraham@sacenter.org
 📞 615-258-5867
 📍 Tennessee Coalition to End Domestic & Sexual Violence
 Brandon Woosley 
 Legal Director 
 📧 info@tncoalition.org
 📞 615-386-9406
 Together, we can help create safer bars, restaurants, and communities across Tennessee. 
 #DrinkSpiking #TennesseeLaw #SafeBars #CommunitySafety #PreventSexualViolence #EndDomesticViolence #EndSexualViolence #SaferTennessee

items.title: CPD and HCSO Share Joint Safety Message Regarding E-Bikes CHATTANOOGA, Tenn. -- The Chattanooga Police Department (CPD)...
items.content_text:
CPD and HCSO Share Joint Safety Message Regarding E-Bikes
 CHATTANOOGA, Tenn. -- The Chattanooga Police Department (CPD) and Hamilton County Sheriff's Office (HCSO) are sharing information regarding E-Bike rules and regulations.
 We know these vehicles are growing in popularity, and many people may not be aware of the legal requirements for operating them. CPD and HCSO are sharing this information now to help keep everyone safe and informed.
 Under Tennessee law, an electric bicycle must generally have fully operable pedals, two or three wheels, and an electric motor of less than 750 watts. E-Bikes must follow all rules of the road when riding on public roads and highways. However, they are not subject to laws or resolutions regarding motor vehicles, such as licensing, registration, and insurance. 
 Electric bicycles are divided into three classes. Here's the differences between each class and their limitations:
 Class 1: Pedal-assist only, with assistance ending at 20 mph. Can ride on any road or path where bicycles are allowed. Cannot ride on sidewalks unless the motor is disabled AND bicycles are allowed there.
 Class 2: Pedal-assist only, with assistance ending at 20 mph. Can ride on any road or path where bicycles are allowed. Cannot ride on sidewalks unless the motor is disabled AND bicycles are allowed there.
 Class 3:  Pedal-assist only, with assistance ending at 28 mph. Can carry a passenger if the vehicle is equipped to do so and the passenger is under the age of 14. Cannot ride on any paths or trails where bicycles are allowed, ride without a speedometer, or be operated without a helmet.
 As of July 1, 2026, Class 3 E-Bike operators must be at least 16-years-old. Other rules of the road for E-Bikes include following all traffic control devices, riding on the right-hand side of the road, using hand signals to communicate, and being equipped with a front white light and a red light or reflector visible for 500 feet. You can learn more at: https://www.tn.gov/…/bicycle-and-pedestrian-program/resourc… 
 We want everyone to enjoy these vehicles, but they must be operated legally, safely, and responsibly. CPD and HCSO will take enforcement action when vehicles are operated unlawfully or in a way that creates a safety risk. Thank you for helping keep our roadways safe. 
 #CPDnews

items.title: The United Kingdom is facing a challenging economic environment. Delivering on the government's reform agenda and mainta...
items.content_text:
The United Kingdom is facing a challenging economic environment. Delivering on the government's reform agenda and maintaining the momentum of reform can help boost productivity, support stronger growth and raise living standards.
 High and volatile energy prices, rising fiscal pressures, low investment and large regional disparities continue to weigh on the economy.
 The #OECD Economic Survey of the United Kingdom identifies reform priorities: 
 ➡️ achieving fiscal sustainability 
 ➡️ ensuring sustainability and adequacy in the pension system 
 ➡️ strengthening energy security 
 ➡️ boosting productivity across regions 
 Today, Åsa Johansson, Director of the Economics department, launched the #OECD Economic Survey of the United Kingdom alongside Torsten Bell MP, Minister for Pensions: https://brnw.ch/21x48Y3

items.title: Honored to represent Hamilton County today for a discussion about infrastructure with my friend Knox County Mayor Glenn...
items.content_text:
Honored to represent Hamilton County today for a discussion about infrastructure with my friend Knox County Mayor Glenn Jacobs, Nashville Mayor Freddie O’Connell, and Memphis Mayor Paul Young. 
 I shared about how we are trying to take a new approach to dealing with growth, preserving and protecting the beauty of rural Hamilton County, while advocating for restorative growth in blighted urban areas, and taking a tougher negotiating position with developers. The group enjoyed hearing about how Gov. Lee and Sen. Watson helped me chart a new course for McDonald Farm, turning it into the newest state park instead of an industrial park.

items.content_text:
As artificial intelligence becomes a bigger part of day-to-day work across industries, the UTC Center for Professional Education will offer two online programs in September to help working professionals build practical AI skills for different needs and experience levels.
 The two offerings are AI Essentials with Prompting, a live online workshop scheduled for Sept. 15 and Sept. 17, and CompTIA Security AI+ Certification Prep, an eight-week live online course that begins Sept. 19. Together, the programs give learners two entry points into AI-related professional development: one focused on broad workplace fluency, productivity and responsible use, and one designed for cybersecurity professionals working in a threat environment increasingly shaped by AI.
 🔗  Read more here: https://blog.utc.edu/…/utc-center-for-professional-educati…/

items.title: It’s official: This Friday, July 17th is Parks and Outdoors Day in Chattanooga!  The City Council passed the resolution...
items.content_text:
It’s official: This Friday, July 17th is Parks and Outdoors Day in Chattanooga! 
 The City Council passed the resolution this afternoon, and we are so grateful to see our team's hard work recognized. From maintaining our neighborhood playgrounds to engaging our youth with programs and camps, our team is out there making sure Chattanooga stays beautiful, safe, and fun for everyone.
 Pictured here is our new Administrator, David Johnson, speaking at the City Council meeting today to advocate for our staff and accept this special recognition on behalf of the whole department. We hope you'll get outside this Friday and enjoy the incredible nature our city has to offer (help us hope for good weather!).
 We want to hear from you: What’s your absolute favorite park, trail, or outdoor spot in Chattanooga? Know a member of our staff who went above and beyond for you? Have a favorite pollinator garden or playground? Let us know in the comments!

items.content_text:
The Health Department’s Radiological Response Team recently participated in two full-scale exercises to demonstrate our readiness to respond during a nuclear power emergency.
 Working alongside local, state, and national partners, the team practiced setting up a community reception center, monitoring individuals, providing decontamination support, and distributing potassium iodide when necessary.
 These exercises help to ensure our team is trained, equipped, and prepared to protect the health of our community.
 We want to thank our partners with the Hamilton County Emergency Management Agency, local schools, and all other partners who helped make this exercise possible.

items.title: Instead of asking whether one vote matters, it may help to ask a different question: Who is showing up? Many Tennesseans...
items.content_text:
Instead of asking whether one vote matters, it may help to ask a different question: Who is showing up?
 Many Tennesseans feel like their vote won't make a difference. That's understandable in a state where statewide outcomes can seem predictable, but local elections often come down to who participates, not just who is registered.
 When turnout is low, a small portion of registered voters end up choosing the people who make decisions about budgets, schools, development, public safety and other local priorities.
 But participation isn't limited to Election Day! Whether you have 15 minutes or 15 hours, there are many ways to help strengthen civic participation and show up for one another (:
 Help us get out the vote this election, sign up for one of our civic actions at the link in our bio!

items.title: TN Reconnect has empowered me to pursue a wonderful goal and has given me a second chance at an education I once thought...
items.content_text:
TN Reconnect has empowered me to pursue a wonderful goal and has given me a second chance at an education I once thought I would never have. 
 My name is Darien, and I am currently pursuing a degree in cybersecurity. 
 Growing up, I didn’t have access to the kind of consistent schooling that many people take for granted, and for a long time, earning a college degree felt completely out of reach.
 The TN Reconnect program changed that for me. It offered not only financial support, but also something far more meaningful —an opportunity. For the first time, I was able to envision myself achieving a higher education and building a future in a field I’m passionate about. TN Reconnect didn’t just open a door; it gave me the means and the confidence to walk through it.
 To future TN Reconnect students, trust yourself, stay patient, and use every resource available to you. Your journey may not look like anyone else’s, and that’s okay. Take each class one step at a time, ask for help when you need it, and remember that it’s never too late to build the life you want. TN Reconnect exists to support people exactly like us — people with dreams, determination, and a desire for something better.
 I am deeply grateful for this program. It truly changes lives, and I am proud to be one of the many Tennesseans who have been able to step forward because of it.
 Learn how TN Reconnect can open doors for you at chattanoogastate.edu/tnreconnect

items.title: Not every stomach bug is the same - knowing the signs can help protect you and your family. Cyclosporiasis is a rare int...
items.content_text:
Not every stomach bug is the same - knowing the signs can help protect you and your family.
 Cyclosporiasis is a rare intestinal illness that can be transmitted through contaminated food or water. According to the CDC, cases of cyclosporiasis have been reported in multiple states across the country, including Tennessee. Although most people recover, symptoms such as diarrhea, stomach cramps, and nausea can last for days or even weeks. 
 To protect yourself, make sure to wash your hands regularly, thoroughly rinse fresh produce, and practice safe food handling. If you are experiencing persistent gastrointestinal symptoms, please contact your healthcare provider. For more information, visit https://www.cdc.gov/cyclosporiasis/index.html & refer to the graphic below.

items.title: Exploring Downtown: The Carnegie Building The Carnegie Building is one of Chattanooga's most storied landmarks. Original...
items.content_text:
Exploring Downtown: The Carnegie Building
 The Carnegie Building is one of Chattanooga's most storied landmarks. Originally built as the Carnegie Library, it is one of more than 2,000 library buildings funded by Scottish-American industrialist and philanthropist Andrew Carnegie. Having left school at age 12, Carnegie credited much of his success to the education he gained through libraries. 
 Construction on Chattanooga's Carnegie Library began in 1903, and the building officially opened to the public in 1905. Although W. T. Bowling, an Atlanta-based architect, was originally hired to design the library, Chattanooga’s most influential architect, R. H. Hunt, was appointed to complete the project after Bowling was discharged. 
 On its opening day, the library and its head librarian, Miss Margaret Dunlap, welcomed around 1,500 people. Guests were greeted not only by the library's impressive collection of books, but also by an orchestra. 
 Today, there are far fewer books in the Carnegie Building than there were a century ago, but its legacy remains closely tied to a movement that expanded access to public libraries in Chattanooga and around the world.

 ⁠The Fall 2026 cycle includes open positions across our Communications, Curatorial, Director’s Office, Development, Education, Graphic Design, Public Programs, and Registrar teams.
